• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Phishing URL Detection — Hypothesis & EDA

K-o-m-A

2026-04-21

library(tidyverse)
library(corrplot)
library(scales)
library(knitr)

1. Problem Statement

1.1 Real-world scenario

A user clicks a link. The browser or a corporate proxy has to decide before the page loads whether the link is phishing. The cheapest signal at that moment is the URL string itself — no DNS lookup, no HTML fetch, no rendering. If a URL-only check is good enough, we block for free; if not, a slower page-level check takes over.

The question: how much phishing signal is already in the URL, and which class of model extracts it best?

1.2 Dataset

PhiUSIIL: ~235 k rows, 54 columns = 5 IDs + label + 48 numeric predictors. After the exclusions in §3.2 we model on 40 predictors split into three families by cost at decision time:

Family What it captures Example features
Lexical Properties of the URL string (zero network cost) URLLength, NoOfSubDomain, NoOfObfuscatedChar, CharContinuationRate
Trust Domain / title consistency (light lookup) IsHTTPS, HasTitle, HasFavicon, Bank, Pay, Crypto
Behavior Page-level content signals (fetch + parse) NoOfURLRedirect, NoOfiFrame, HasExternalFormSubmit, HasHiddenFields

Lexical is the URL-only first-line case. Trust and Behavior require a network fetch and act as the rich-feature upper bound.

2. Hypothesis

H1 — On the URL-only tier, non-parametric models pick up meaningfully more signal than parametric ones

Phishing URLs usually leave lexical traces: odd subdomain depth, digit-heavy paths, obfuscated characters. Lexical patterns interact in non-additive ways (length × digit count × special chars), so a linear decision boundary cannot fully use those interactions. We compare three parametric methods (LR, LDA, NB) against three non-parametric ones (RF, SVM-RBF, KNN) on the Lexical tier, and repeat on a richer tier as a saturation check.

Success criteria (tested in scenario_2.rmd with a 5-fold CV protocol on shared folds):

# Criterion Threshold
C1 Mean CV AUC gap between the best non-parametric and best parametric model on Lexical ΔAUC ≥ 0.02
C2 Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on the 5 per-fold AUCs of those two models p < 0.05
C3 Gradient: Lexical gap strictly larger than rich-tier gap gap(Lexical) > gap(FullLite)

H1 is supported only if C1 and C2 hold. C3 turns the claim into “the non-parametric advantage shrinks as the feature tier gets stronger.”

3. Data Loading & Cleaning

raw <- read_csv("PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
names(raw) <- map_chr(names(raw), ~ str_replace(.x, "^\ufeff", ""))
ID_COLS <- c("FILENAME", "URL", "Domain", "TLD", "Title")

df <- raw %>%
  mutate(label_bin = factor(label, levels = c(0, 1),
                            labels = c("Legitimate", "Phishing"))) %>%
  select(-all_of(ID_COLS))

features <- df %>% select(-label, -label_bin)

cat("Observations:", nrow(df), "\n")
Observations: 235795 
cat("Predictors (before exclusion):", ncol(features), "\n")
Predictors (before exclusion): 50

3.1 Family map

Families reflect the cost of obtaining each signal at decision time. IsHTTPS is placed in Trust, not Lexical: modern browsers refuse plain HTTP for sensitive interactions, so the scheme is enforced at the trust layer rather than acting as a free lexical clue.

family_map <- tribble(
  ~feature,                    ~family,
  "URLLength",                 "Lexical",
  "DomainLength",              "Lexical",
  "IsDomainIP",                "Lexical",
  "URLSimilarityIndex",        "Trust",
  "CharContinuationRate",      "Lexical",
  "TLDLegitimateProb",         "Trust",
  "URLCharProb",               "Trust",
  "TLDLength",                 "Lexical",
  "NoOfSubDomain",             "Lexical",
  "HasObfuscation",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfObfuscatedChar",        "Lexical",
  "ObfuscationRatio",          "Lexical",
  "NoOfLettersInURL",          "Lexical",
  "LetterRatioInURL",          "Lexical",
  "NoOfDegitsInURL",           "Lexical",
  "DegitRatioInURL",           "Lexical",
  "NoOfEqualsInURL",           "Lexical",
  "NoOfQMarkInURL",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfAmpersandInURL",        "Lexical",
  "NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL","Lexical",
  "SpacialCharRatioInURL",     "Lexical",
  "IsHTTPS",                   "Trust",
  "LineOfCode",                "Behavior",
  "LargestLineLength",         "Behavior",
  "HasTitle",                  "Trust",
  "DomainTitleMatchScore",     "Trust",
  "URLTitleMatchScore",        "Trust",
  "HasFavicon",                "Trust",
  "Robots",                    "Trust",
  "IsResponsive",              "Behavior",
  "NoOfURLRedirect",           "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRedirect",          "Behavior",
  "HasDescription",            "Behavior",
  "NoOfPopup",                 "Behavior",
  "NoOfiFrame",                "Behavior",
  "HasExternalFormSubmit",     "Behavior",
  "HasSocialNet",              "Behavior",
  "HasSubmitButton",           "Behavior",
  "HasHiddenFields",           "Behavior",
  "HasPasswordField",          "Behavior",
  "Bank",                      "Trust",
  "Pay",                       "Trust",
  "Crypto",                    "Trust",
  "HasCopyrightInfo",          "Behavior",
  "NoOfImage",                 "Behavior",
  "NoOfCSS",                   "Behavior",
  "NoOfJS",                    "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRef",               "Behavior",
  "NoOfEmptyRef",              "Behavior",
  "NoOfExternalRef",           "Behavior"
)

3.2 Feature exclusion

Three groups of features are dropped before modelling.

(a) Computed scores — outputs of other models or hand-crafted scoring functions that already encode phishing logic. Keeping them means classifying a classifier’s output (e.g. URLSimilarityIndex alone gives AUC ≈ 1.0).

SCORES <- c("URLSimilarityIndex", "TLDLegitimateProb", "URLCharProb",
            "DomainTitleMatchScore", "URLTitleMatchScore")

(b) Redundant binary flag. HasObfuscation = (NoOfObfuscatedChar > 0) — a deterministic function of a count already in the dataset.

REDUNDANT_BINARY <- "HasObfuscation"

(c) Redundant ratios. Each of these equals count / URLLength where both numerator and denominator are already present. We keep the raw counts (easier units to interpret) and drop the ratio.

RATIOS <- c("LetterRatioInURL", "DegitRatioInURL",
            "ObfuscationRatio", "SpacialCharRatioInURL")
EXCLUDE_COLS <- c(SCORES, REDUNDANT_BINARY, RATIOS)

df       <- df       %>% select(-all_of(EXCLUDE_COLS))
features <- df       %>% select(-label, -label_bin)
family_map <- family_map %>% filter(!feature %in% EXCLUDE_COLS)

cat("Features after exclusion:", ncol(features),
    "(dropped", length(EXCLUDE_COLS), "/", ncol(features) + length(EXCLUDE_COLS), ")\n")
Features after exclusion: 40 (dropped 10 / 50 )
family_map %>% count(family) %>% print()
# A tibble: 3 × 2
  family       n
  <chr>    <int>
1 Behavior    20
2 Lexical     13
3 Trust        7

3.3 Feature tiers used in modelling

tier_lexical  <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Lexical")  %>% pull(feature)
tier_trust    <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Trust")    %>% pull(feature)
tier_behavior <- family_map %>% filter(family == "Behavior") %>% pull(feature)
tier_full     <- c(tier_lexical, tier_trust, tier_behavior)

cat("Lexical (URLOnly):", length(tier_lexical), "features\n")
Lexical (URLOnly): 13 features
cat("Trust:            ", length(tier_trust),   "features\n")
Trust:             7 features
cat("Behavior:         ", length(tier_behavior),"features\n")
Behavior:          20 features
cat("Full:             ", length(tier_full),    "features\n")
Full:              40 features

4. Exploratory Data Analysis

Every plot in this section is tied to a specific modelling decision — we avoid generic per-feature histograms or a global correlation matrix across all 40 predictors, following the assignment’s guidance to keep EDA deliberate rather than exhaustive.

4.1 Class balance and missingness

df %>%
  count(label_bin) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(label_bin, n, fill = label_bin)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(pct, 0.1)), vjust = -0.4, size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("steelblue", "firebrick")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, .1))) +
  labs(title = "Class distribution", x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

cat("Total NAs in feature matrix:", sum(is.na(features)), "\n")
Total NAs in feature matrix: 0

Roughly balanced (≈57/43) → AUC and Accuracy are both valid metrics, no resampling needed. Zero missing values in any predictor. Scenario 2 additionally stratifies the modelling sub-sample to an exact 50/50, so class weighting is not needed at either stage.

4.2 Per-feature discriminative power by family

If no single Lexical feature separates the classes, the model has to combine several of them — the setting where non-parametric models tend to beat linear/Gaussian baselines.

We compute discriminative power separately for continuous and binary features. SMD is only meaningful for continuous features; for binary features the pooled SD degenerates, so we use Cramér’s V on a 2×2 contingency table (same 0–1 scale as |correlation|).

smd_df <- df %>%
  select(label, all_of(continuous_features)) %>%
  pivot_longer(-label, names_to = "feature", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(feature, label) %>%
  summarise(m = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), s = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = label, values_from = c(m, s),
              names_glue = "{.value}_{label}") %>%
  mutate(pooled_sd = sqrt((s_0^2 + s_1^2) / 2),
         effect    = abs(m_1 - m_0) / (pooled_sd + 1e-9),
         metric    = "SMD (continuous)") %>%
  select(feature, effect, metric)

cramers_v <- function(x, y) {
  tab <- table(x, y)
  if (any(dim(tab) < 2)) return(NA_real_)
  chi2 <- suppressWarnings(chisq.test(tab, correct = FALSE)$statistic)
  as.numeric(sqrt(chi2 / (sum(tab) * (min(dim(tab)) - 1))))
}

cv_df <- tibble(
  feature = binary_features,
  effect  = map_dbl(binary_features, ~ cramers_v(features[[.x]], df$label)),
  metric  = "Cramer's V (binary)"
)

discrim_df <- bind_rows(smd_df, cv_df) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other"))

ggplot(discrim_df, aes(reorder(feature, effect), effect, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() + coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~ metric, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Per-feature discriminative power by family",
       subtitle = "Continuous: |SMD|, Binary: Cramer's V. Both >= 0.",
       x = NULL, y = "Effect size", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

Takeaway. Lexical features cluster at the bottom of the |SMD| ranking — no individual URL-length or digit-count feature separates classes on its own. Trust and Behavior carry stronger per-feature signal. Weak per-feature evidence means a Lexical classifier must combine many small cues, which is the setting in which tree ensembles and kernel methods typically beat linear models — the motivation for H1.

4.3 Multicollinearity — preparation for modelling

The Lexical family contains a dense count cluster (URLLength, NoOfLettersInURL, NoOfDegitsInURL, and the obfuscation / equals / ampersand triangle). Plain (unpenalised) logistic regression is numerically unstable on such a cluster, so Scenario 2 uses ridge-regularised LR on every tier. We document the cluster here via a correlation plot and target-free VIF so the modelling decision is traceable to data.

lexical_cor <- features %>% select(all_of(tier_lexical)) %>%
  cor(use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

corrplot(lexical_cor, method = "color", order = "hclust",
         tl.cex = 0.7, tl.col = "black",
         col = colorRampPalette(c("firebrick", "white", "steelblue"))(200),
         title = "Correlation — Lexical features",
         mar = c(0, 0, 1.5, 0),
         addCoef.col = "black", number.cex = 0.55)

Target-free VIF — regress each continuous feature on the rest of the design matrix. This avoids the non-converged binomial GLM that car::vif produces on this dataset and is computed directly from lm:

X_vif <- features %>% select(all_of(continuous_features)) %>% as.data.frame()

vif_values <- vapply(seq_along(X_vif), function(i) {
  r2 <- summary(lm(X_vif[[i]] ~ ., data = X_vif[, -i, drop = FALSE]))$r.squared
  1 / (1 - r2)
}, numeric(1))
names(vif_values) <- names(X_vif)

tibble(feature = names(vif_values), vif = as.numeric(vif_values)) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  mutate(severity = case_when(vif > 10 ~ "severe (> 10)",
                              vif >= 5 ~ "moderate (5-10)",
                              TRUE     ~ "ok (< 5)")) %>%
  arrange(desc(vif)) %>%
  transmute(Feature = feature, Family = family,
            VIF = round(vif, 2), Severity = severity) %>%
  kable(caption = "Variance Inflation Factors (lm-based, target-free).")
Variance Inflation Factors (lm-based, target-free).
Feature Family VIF Severity
URLLength Lexical 1070.20 severe (> 10)
NoOfLettersInURL Lexical 535.40 severe (> 10)
NoOfDegitsInURL Lexical 90.60 severe (> 10)
NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL Lexical 10.16 severe (> 10)
NoOfEqualsInURL Lexical 7.94 moderate (5-10)
NoOfObfuscatedChar Lexical 4.90 ok (< 5)
NoOfAmpersandInURL Lexical 3.18 ok (< 5)
CharContinuationRate Lexical 2.77 ok (< 5)
NoOfSelfRef Behavior 2.19 ok (< 5)
NoOfExternalRef Behavior 1.98 ok (< 5)
NoOfSubDomain Lexical 1.95 ok (< 5)
NoOfQMarkInURL Lexical 1.86 ok (< 5)
DomainLength Lexical 1.80 ok (< 5)
LineOfCode Behavior 1.38 ok (< 5)
NoOfImage Behavior 1.34 ok (< 5)
NoOfJS Behavior 1.13 ok (< 5)
NoOfiFrame Behavior 1.11 ok (< 5)
TLDLength Lexical 1.11 ok (< 5)
NoOfCSS Behavior 1.08 ok (< 5)
NoOfEmptyRef Behavior 1.03 ok (< 5)
LargestLineLength Behavior 1.01 ok (< 5)
NoOfPopup Behavior 1.01 ok (< 5)

Takeaway. URLLength, NoOfLettersInURL and NoOfDegitsInURL remain near-collinear after the ratio-feature drop because they measure overlapping properties of the same string. This is what motivates ridge-regularised LR in Scenario 2; unpenalised LR would swing its coefficients on this cluster from fold to fold.

4.4 Skewness

skewness_fn <- function(x) {
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]; n <- length(x); m <- mean(x); s <- sd(x)
  (n / ((n-1)*(n-2))) * sum(((x - m) / s)^3)
}

skew_df <- tibble(
  feature  = continuous_features,
  skewness = map_dbl(continuous_features, ~ skewness_fn(features[[.x]]))
) %>%
  left_join(family_map, by = "feature") %>%
  replace_na(list(family = "Other")) %>%
  mutate(abs_skew = abs(skewness))

ggplot(skew_df, aes(reorder(feature, abs_skew), skewness, fill = family)) +
  geom_col() + coord_flip() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 2), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Skewness of continuous features",
       subtitle = "Dashed lines at +/- 2 mark heavy skew",
       x = NULL, y = "Skewness", fill = "Family") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

cat("Features with |skewness| > 2:", sum(skew_df$abs_skew > 2),
    "of", length(continuous_features), "\n")
Features with |skewness| > 2: 19 of 22

Takeaway. Most continuous features are heavily right-skewed (count data with long tails). Parametric LR/LDA/NB and distance-based KNN/SVM apply log1p + centre/scale in Scenario 2; Random Forest is left untransformed.

5. EDA summary

After exclusions: 40 features = 13 Lexical + 7 Trust + 20 Behavior.

Finding Modelling implication
Roughly balanced (57/43), zero NAs AUC + Accuracy both valid; Scenario 2 stratifies sub-sample to exact 50/50
Per-feature SMD lowest on Lexical Largest parametric/non-parametric gap expected on Lexical — the H1 test
Heavy skew on 19/22 continuous features log1p + centre/scale for LR/LDA/NB/SVM/KNN; trees unaffected
URL-length cluster with VIF > 500 Ridge-regularised LR in Scenario 2
Full-tier saturates at AUC ≈ 0.99 Compare models on Lexical (where the gap has room to show) and on a >30-feature “lite” tier that drops the near-leaky page counts

Scenario 2 reuses the same exclusions and tier definitions, so the findings above apply directly to the modelling results there.
